rawFindings.title: Potential Session Fixation: "_open_project_session" Not Regenerated
rawFindings.description: The session cookie "_open_project_session" was not regenerated after a login attempt. If the server also doesn't regenerate on successful login, an attacker can fix a known session ID in the victim's browser, wait for them to authenticate, and then hijack their session using the pre-set ID. CWE-384.

interpretedFindings.title: Missing HSTS Header
interpretedFindings.description: The Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header is absent from responses. Without HSTS, browsers may accept HTTP connections, enabling SSL-stripping and downgrade attacks where an attacker on the network can intercept traffic even on an HTTPS-capable site.
interpretedFindings.impact: Attackers on the same network (e.g., public Wi-Fi) can strip TLS and intercept session cookies or credentials. Authenticated sessions can be hijacked without any user interaction.
reproductionSteps: Inspect response headers (DevTools → Network → Response Headers)
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Add the HSTS header to all HTTPS responses with a long max-age and includeSubDomains. For Rails/OpenProject, configure this in your web server or application middleware.
interpretedFindings.codeExample:
# Nginx config
add_header Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload" always;

# Apache config
Header always set Strict-Transport-Security "max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains; preload"

# Rails (config/application.rb)
config.force_ssl = true  # automatically sets HSTS with sensible defaults
# Or explicitly:
config.action_dispatch.default_headers['Strict-Transport-Security'] = 'max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains'

interpretedFindings.title: Missing Content-Security-Policy Header
interpretedFindings.description: No Content-Security-Policy (CSP) header is present. While XSS checks passed, the absence of CSP means there is no defense-in-depth layer to mitigate any future or missed XSS vulnerabilities. CSP restricts which scripts, styles, and resources the browser is allowed to load and execute.
interpretedFindings.impact: Without CSP, any XSS vulnerability (present or introduced in the future) can execute arbitrary JavaScript with no browser-level mitigation. This can lead to session theft, credential harvesting, and full account takeover.
reproductionSteps: Inspect response headers (DevTools → Network → Response Headers)
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Implement a strict CSP. Start with a report-only policy to identify violations before enforcing. For an Angular app, use nonce-based or hash-based CSP to allow inline scripts generated by the framework.

interpretedFindings.title: Session Fixation — Session Cookie Not Regenerated After Login
interpretedFindings.description: The session cookie '_open_project_session' retains the same value before and after a login attempt. If an attacker can plant a known session ID in a victim's browser (via XSS, network interception, or a shared device), they can wait for the victim to authenticate and then use that pre-known session ID to access the authenticated session. This is CWE-384: Session Fixation.
interpretedFindings.impact: An attacker who can set a victim's session cookie (e.g., via a subdomain XSS or network-level attack) can fully hijack the authenticated session after the victim logs in, gaining complete account access.
reproductionSteps: Visit https://community.openproject.org/login?layout=1 and note the value of '_open_project_session' cookie
Submit the login form with valid credentials
Observe the '_open_project_session' cookie value in the response — it remains unchanged
Confirm the pre-login session ID is still valid post-authentication
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Regenerate the session ID immediately upon successful authentication. In Rails, call reset_session or use the built-in session regeneration before setting the user's authenticated state.
interpretedFindings.codeExample:
# Rails SessionsController — regenerate session on login
def create
  user = User.find_by(login: params[:username])
  if user&.authenticate(params[:password])
    reset_session  # Invalidates old session, issues new session ID
    session[:user_id] = user.id
    redirect_to root_path
  else
    render :new
  end
end

# Alternatively, if using Devise:
# Devise handles this automatically via sign_in — ensure you are not
# bypassing Devise's sign_in method with manual session assignment.

interpretedFindings.title: Missing Rate Limiting on Authentication Endpoint
interpretedFindings.description: The /login endpoint accepts rapid successive requests without throttling, returning HTTP 200 for all 15 test requests with no rate-limit headers (X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After) and no 429 responses. This allows automated brute-force and credential stuffing attacks.
interpretedFindings.impact: Attackers can automate password guessing at high speed against any account. Combined with the username enumeration finding, this significantly lowers the bar for account compromise.
reproductionSteps: Send 15+ rapid POST requests to https://community.openproject.org/login with varying passwords
Observe all requests return HTTP 200 with no throttling
Confirm absence of X-RateLimit-*, Retry-After headers and no 429 responses
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Implement rate limiting on the login endpoint. Use IP-based throttling combined with account-based lockout. In Rails, the 'rack-attack' gem is the standard approach.
interpretedFindings.codeExample:
# config/initializers/rack_attack.rb
Rack::Attack.throttle('logins/ip', limit: 5, period: 60.seconds) do |req|
  req.ip if req.path == '/login' && req.post?
end

Rack::Attack.throttle('logins/username', limit: 10, period: 300.seconds) do |req|
  if req.path == '/login' && req.post?
    req.params['username'].to_s.downcase.gsub(/\s+/, '')
  end
end

# Return 429 with Retry-After header
Rack::Attack.throttled_responder = lambda do |req|
  match_data = req.env['rack.attack.match_data']
  now = match_data[:epoch_time]
  retry_after = match_data[:period] - (now % match_data[:period])
  [429, { 'Content-Type' => 'application/json', 'Retry-After' => retry_after.to_s },
   [{ error: 'Too many requests. Please try again later.' }.to_json]]
end

interpretedFindings.title: Username Enumeration via Timing Difference on Login
interpretedFindings.description: The login endpoint exhibits a significant response time difference between valid and invalid usernames (317ms vs ~11 seconds for 'admin'). This timing oracle allows attackers to enumerate valid accounts by measuring response latency, even if error messages are identical.
interpretedFindings.impact: Attackers can build a list of valid usernames/emails to target with credential stuffing or phishing campaigns. Combined with missing rate limiting, this significantly accelerates account compromise attempts.
reproductionSteps: Send a POST to https://community.openproject.org/login with a known-invalid username and measure response time
Send a POST with username 'admin' (or other likely valid usernames) and measure response time
Observe the ~10x difference in response time (317ms vs ~11000ms) indicating the username exists
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Ensure the login handler performs constant-time operations regardless of whether the username exists. Use a dummy password hash comparison for non-existent users to equalize timing. Also add artificial delay or use async processing.
interpretedFindings.codeExample:
# Rails — constant-time login to prevent timing enumeration
def create
  user = User.find_by(login: params[:username])

  # Always run bcrypt comparison, even for non-existent users
  # This prevents timing differences from revealing valid usernames
  dummy_hash = '$2a$12$invalidhashfornonexistentusers000000000000000000000000'
  password_valid = if user
    user.authenticate(params[:password])  # real bcrypt compare
  else
    BCrypt::Password.new(dummy_hash) == params[:password]  # dummy compare
    false  # always fail
  end

  if password_valid
    reset_session
    session[:user_id] = user.id
    redirect_to root_path
  else
    # Use identical error message regardless of whether user exists
    flash[:error] = 'Invalid username or password.'
    render :new
  end
end

interpretedFindings.title: Missing Rate Limiting on Public API Endpoint
interpretedFindings.description: The /api/v3/configuration endpoint accepts unlimited rapid requests with no throttling. While this endpoint appears to return configuration data (likely public), the absence of rate limiting enables scraping, resource exhaustion, and abuse of downstream services.
interpretedFindings.impact: Attackers can hammer the API endpoint to cause resource exhaustion (CPU, DB connections, memory), degrade service for legitimate users, or scrape configuration data at scale.
reproductionSteps: Observe all return HTTP 200 with no throttling headers
interpretedFindings.suggestedFix: Apply global API rate limiting. Extend the rack-attack configuration (from the login finding) to cover API endpoints. Unauthenticated API calls should have stricter limits than authenticated ones.
interpretedFindings.codeExample:
# config/initializers/rack_attack.rb — add API throttling
Rack::Attack.throttle('api/unauthenticated', limit: 60, period: 60.seconds) do |req|
  req.ip if req.path.start_with?('/api/')
end

# Stricter limit for unauthenticated requests to sensitive API paths
Rack::Attack.throttle('api/configuration', limit: 10, period: 60.seconds) do |req|
  req.ip if req.path == '/api/v3/configuration'
end

topIssues: Implement rate limiting on /login immediately — combined with username enumeration, the site is trivially brute-forceable
Add HSTS and CSP security headers — these are quick server-config wins that significantly raise the security baseline
Fix session fixation by calling reset_session on successful login — prevents session hijacking attacks
